name: Bird
summary: Electric scooter-sharing pioneer that scaled rapidly across hundreds of cities but never achieved sustainable unit economics; filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in December 2023.
lesson: Rapid geographic expansion without solving vehicle durability and repair costs burns cash faster than growth can offset.

name: Jump Bikes
summary: Electric bike-share startup (originally Social Bicycles) acquired by Uber in 2018; Uber shut down and sold off Jump's operations in 2020 amid COVID-driven cost cuts.
lesson: A promising acquisition can still be unwound when the parent company's own priorities shift.

name: Skip Scooters
summary: San Francisco-based e-scooter operator that lost several city permits and was later acquired by Helbiz, which itself unwound the business.
lesson: Permit-dependent micromobility businesses live and die by city regulatory relationships.

name: Spin
summary: E-scooter startup acquired by Ford in 2018, then sold to Tier Mobility in 2022 after Ford failed to make micromobility work within its business.
lesson: Corporate-owned mobility units often get deprioritized and divested when parent strategy shifts.

name: Wheels
summary: Seated e-moped/scooter startup that was acquired by Helbiz in 2022 as the broader micromobility sector consolidated amid financing pressure.
lesson: An oversaturated scooter market forced consolidation among undifferentiated players.

name: Zagster
summary: Early bike-share operator serving campuses and cities that sold its assets to Superpedestrian's Spin brand in 2019 after failing to compete with dockless scooter rivals.
lesson: Station-based bike-share models struggled to compete once dockless scooters offered more convenience.

name: Superpedestrian
summary: MIT-spinout maker of the LINK scooter with proprietary safety tech that shut down its micromobility operations in late 2023 after failing to raise further funding.
lesson: Even differentiated hardware-safety technology couldn't overcome the capital intensity of scooter-fleet operations.

name: Ofo
summary: Chinese dockless bike-share giant that expanded to 20+ countries but collapsed under debt, deposit-refund scandals, and cutthroat competition with Mobike, retreating from most markets by 2019.
lesson: Blitzscaling into dozens of countries simultaneously without a path to profitability invites collapse.

name: Mobike
summary: Chinese dockless bike-share company that won the brutal bike-share war with Ofo and was acquired by Meituan for about $2.7 billion in 2018, a rare profitable exit in the sector.
lesson: Being acquired by a super-app before losing a war of attrition can turn a capital-intensive business into a real win.

name: Helbiz
summary: NYC-based micromobility company that went public via SPAC in 2021, acquired multiple failing scooter rivals, but was delisted from Nasdaq in 2024 amid steep losses.
lesson: Rolling up other struggling scooter companies compounds losses rather than fixing the underlying economics.

name: Tier Mobility
summary: European scooter and e-bike sharing leader that acquired Spin in 2022 but filed for insolvency proceedings in 2024 after failing to reach profitability at scale.
lesson: Market leadership in micromobility doesn't guarantee profitability when vehicle replacement costs stay high.

name: Circ
summary: Berlin-based e-scooter startup (rebranded from Flash) acquired by Bird in 2020 as the European scooter market consolidated rapidly.
lesson: In a saturated market, being acquired by a larger rival is often the only viable exit.

name: Gotcha Mobility
summary: South Carolina-based micromobility and car-share operator serving smaller college towns that ceased operations in 2023 as funding dried up.
lesson: Serving smaller secondary markets doesn't insulate a mobility startup from the same capital constraints as big-city rivals.

name: Sidecar
summary: Early ride-hailing and courier pioneer that introduced features later copied by Uber and Lyft but shut down its ride service in 2015, selling assets to General Motors.
lesson: Being first with an innovative feature doesn't matter if better-funded competitors can copy it and outspend you.

name: Karhoo
summary: London-based ride-hailing marketplace aggregating taxis and private-hire cars that collapsed into administration in 2017 just months after a high-profile US launch.
lesson: Aggressive multi-country expansion funded by debt-like structures can implode quickly when investor confidence wavers.

name: Summon
summary: Los Angeles-based ride-hailing startup that attempted to compete directly with Uber and Lyft in a single metro market and shut down within two years.
lesson: Regional ride-hailing challengers rarely survive head-on competition against nationally-scaled incumbents.

name: Getaround
summary: Peer-to-peer car-sharing marketplace that went public via SPAC in 2022 at a rich valuation but saw its stock collapse over 99%, leading to a Nasdaq delisting in 2024.
lesson: A SPAC listing can mask unresolved unit-economics problems that eventually surface in public markets.

name: Car2Go / Share Now
summary: Daimler's free-floating car-sharing service merged with BMW's DriveNow to form Share Now in 2019, exited North America the same year, and the whole venture shut down in early 2024.
lesson: Free-floating car-sharing fleets require fleet-scale capital that even automakers found unsustainable long term.

name: ReachNow
summary: BMW's US car-sharing and ride-hailing service launched in Seattle and other cities but was shut down in 2019 as BMW consolidated mobility efforts into Share Now.
lesson: Automaker mobility spinoffs often get folded or killed when parent-company strategy consolidates.

name: Maven
summary: General Motors' car-sharing brand operating in over a dozen US cities was shut down in 2019 as GM redirected investment toward Cruise's autonomous vehicle efforts.
lesson: Corporate mobility experiments get cut quickly when a flashier bet (like autonomy) captures leadership's attention.

name: Fair.com
summary: Flexible car-leasing/subscription startup led by former Uber and CreditKarma executives that laid off most of its staff in 2019 and effectively collapsed after burning through massive debt financing.
lesson: Using large amounts of debt to subsidize a subscription vehicle model magnifies losses when demand assumptions are wrong.

name: Argo AI
summary: Self-driving car startup backed by Ford and Volkswagen with a $7B+ valuation that was shut down in October 2022 after both automakers concluded full autonomy was still too far off to justify continued investment.
lesson: Even multi-billion-dollar backing can't save an AV startup once corporate patrons lose patience with the technology timeline.

name: Cruise
summary: GM's robotaxi subsidiary suspended all driverless operations nationwide in late 2023 after a pedestrian dragging incident in San Francisco, leading to major layoffs, leadership departures, and GM later halting robotaxi development entirely in 2024.
lesson: A single serious safety incident can unravel years of regulatory goodwill and force a strategic retreat from autonomy.

name: Starsky Robotics
summary: Autonomous trucking startup that achieved a driverless highway run in 2019 but shut down in 2020 after failing to raise a new funding round, citing investor skepticism toward pure self-driving plays.
lesson: Technical milestones don't guarantee fundability when investor sentiment toward a whole subsector sours.

name: Ike
summary: Autonomous trucking startup founded by former Google and Otto engineers that was acquired by Nuro in 2022 primarily for its engineering talent, ending as an independent company.
lesson: Talent-acquisition exits in capital-intensive AV trucking are common when independent scaling proves too costly.

name: TuSimple
summary: Autonomous trucking company that IPO'd in 2021 but was delisted from Nasdaq in 2024 amid a federal fraud investigation, executive turnover, and a pivot away from its core US trucking business.
lesson: Governance scandals and leadership instability can destroy an AV company's public-market credibility even after a successful IPO.

name: Embark Trucks
summary: Publicly-traded autonomous trucking startup that shut down operations and sold off its assets and IP in 2023 after running out of runway to reach commercial deployment.
lesson: Long AV development timelines routinely outlast the funding cycles available to sustain them.

name: Drive.ai
summary: Self-driving startup known for its distinctive orange vans and Texas robotaxi pilots that ran low on funding and was acquired by Apple in 2019 mainly for engineering talent.
lesson: A visible pilot program isn't the same as a fundable path to commercial-scale autonomy.

name: Voyage
summary: Self-driving taxi startup focused on retirement communities that was acquired by Cruise in 2021, ending its run as an independent AV company.
lesson: Niche AV deployment strategies (like retirement communities) can prove a concept but rarely generate independent scale.

name: Fisker Inc. (2nd company)
summary: Henrik Fisker's second EV venture launched the Ocean SUV but filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in June 2024 after severe production quality issues, plunging sales, and inability to raise further capital.
lesson: Rushing a complex vehicle to market without a mature manufacturing and quality process leads to a death spiral of recalls and returns.

name: Fisker Automotive (1st company)
summary: Henrik Fisker's first EV company built the Karma plug-in hybrid but collapsed into bankruptcy in 2013 after battery supplier A123's bankruptcy, manufacturing delays, and a government loan default.
lesson: Overreliance on a single battery supplier creates existential single-point-of-failure risk for an EV startup.

name: Faraday Future
summary: China-backed luxury EV startup that promised to rival Tesla but has produced only a tiny number of its FF 91 vehicles amid years of near-bankruptcy, executive turnover, and fraud allegations from the SEC.
lesson: Grandiose promises without manufacturing discipline erode investor and consumer trust long before a product ships at scale.

name: Better Place
summary: Israeli EV infrastructure startup that built battery-swap stations betting automakers would adopt its standard; it collapsed into bankruptcy in 2013 after burning through nearly a billion dollars with minimal adoption.
lesson: Building expensive infrastructure around a proprietary standard is fatal if automakers never commit to it.

name: Coda Automotive
summary: Los Angeles-based EV sedan startup that sold fewer than 100 cars before filing for bankruptcy in 2013 due to poor product quality and inability to raise further capital.
lesson: A poorly reviewed debut product can doom an EV startup before it ever reaches meaningful volume.

name: Lordstown Motors
summary: Electric pickup truck startup that went public via SPAC in 2020 but filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2023 after a canceled GM investment and fraud allegations over its pre-order numbers.
lesson: Inflating early demand metrics to support a SPAC valuation invites lawsuits and investor flight once the truth surfaces.

name: Canoo
summary: EV startup known for its modular 'skateboard' platform that went public via SPAC in 2020, repeatedly restructured, and filed for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in 2025 after years of production delays and cash shortfalls.
lesson: A distinctive platform design doesn't substitute for the manufacturing capital needed to actually build cars at scale.

name: Electric Last Mile Solutions
summary: Electric delivery van maker that went public via SPAC in 2021 but filed for bankruptcy just over a year later in 2022 amid an accounting scandal over founder stock issuances.
lesson: SPAC-era EV startups with weak governance controls were especially vulnerable to founder self-dealing scandals.

name: Nikola Corporation
summary: Hydrogen and electric truck maker that went public via SPAC in 2020 at an eye-popping valuation, was later found by short-seller Hindenburg Research to have faked a demo video, saw founder Trevor Milton convicted of fraud, and filed for bankruptcy in 2025.
lesson: Fabricating product demonstrations to inflate a valuation is a criminal liability, not just a PR risk.

name: Arrival
summary: UK-based electric van and bus maker known for its 'microfactory' manufacturing concept that went public via SPAC in 2021 but filed for bankruptcy in 2024 after failing to reach production at scale.
lesson: Novel micro-factory manufacturing concepts still need to prove out at commercial volume before investors' patience runs out.

name: Bollinger Motors
summary: Electric truck and commercial van startup that filed for Chapter 7 bankruptcy liquidation in 2024 after years of delayed production and failed partnership attempts, including with Mullen Automotive.
lesson: Commercial EV makers need deep-pocketed manufacturing partners; failed partnership talks can be a fatal blow.

name: Byton
summary: China-based EV startup founded by ex-BMW and Nissan executives that showcased a futuristic SUV concept for years but never reached mass production and formally dissolved in 2023.
lesson: Years of flashy concept demos can't substitute for an actual production line and delivered vehicles.

name: Local Motors
summary: Pioneer of 3D-printed and open-source vehicle manufacturing, including the Olli autonomous shuttle, that abruptly shut down in 2022 after failing to close a new funding round.
lesson: Innovative manufacturing techniques alone don't create a sustainable business without a clear path to volume revenue.

name: Proterra
summary: Electric bus and battery maker that went public via SPAC in 2021 but filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2023 due to rising costs, supply chain issues, and cash burn, later selling its bus unit to Phoenix Motor.
lesson: Even a well-regarded electric bus product with real transit customers can be undone by manufacturing cost overruns.

name: Volta Trucks
summary: Swedish electric commercial truck maker that filed for insolvency in late 2023 after its battery supplier Proterra went bankrupt, disrupting Volta's own production ramp.
lesson: Supply-chain dependency risk cascades: one supplier's bankruptcy can trigger a customer's collapse too.

name: Luxe
summary: On-demand valet parking startup operating in several major US cities that shut down its consumer parking service in 2017, with Volvo licensing its technology afterward.
lesson: On-demand valet labor costs proved too high relative to what urban parkers were willing to pay.

name: Zirx
summary: On-demand valet parking startup backed by Trinity Ventures and GV that shut down operations in 2017 after struggling to find a profitable business model.
lesson: Two well-funded valet-parking startups (Luxe and Zirx) failing in the same year signaled the category itself was structurally unprofitable.

name: Chariot
summary: Crowdsourced commuter shuttle-van startup acquired by Ford in 2016 that was shut down in 2019 as Ford Smart Mobility cut underperforming units amid a broader restructuring.
lesson: Shuttle-van services face brutal per-seat economics that scale poorly even under automaker ownership.

name: Bridj
summary: On-demand shuttle bus startup piloted in Boston, Kansas City and Sydney that shut down in 2017 after failing to find a sustainable model and losing a major transit-agency partnership.
lesson: Public transit-agency partnerships can be a lifeline or a liability depending on how dependent the startup's revenue is on them.

name: Leap Transit
summary: San Francisco luxury commuter bus startup shut down by regulators in 2015 for operating without proper permits, less than a year after launch.
lesson: Skipping local transit permitting requirements to move fast can end a company almost overnight.

name: Shuddle
summary: 'Uber for kids' ride-hailing startup providing vetted drivers for unaccompanied minors that shut down in 2016 after failing to raise a follow-on funding round.
lesson: Trust-and-safety-heavy niches like child transportation require expensive vetting infrastructure that's hard to fund at startup speed.

name: Silvercar
summary: Premium airport rental-car startup (all-silver Audi A4 fleet) that Audi acquired in 2017 for an estimated $300M+, then shut down entirely in 2019 as Audi refocused its mobility strategy.
lesson: A lucrative acquisition exit doesn't guarantee long-term survival once the acquirer's strategic priorities change.

name: FlightCar
summary: Airport car-sharing startup letting travelers rent out their cars while away that shut down in 2017 amid insurance disputes, lawsuits, and unsustainable operating losses.
lesson: Insurance and liability complexity in peer-to-peer vehicle sharing can create legal exposure that outpaces revenue.

name: Convoy
summary: Digital freight-matching marketplace once valued near $3.8B that abruptly shut down in October 2023 after a stalled freight market and inability to raise emergency funding.
lesson: Freight-tech marketplaces are highly cyclical; a prolonged freight recession can sink even a top-tier funded player.

name: Virgin Hyperloop
summary: Richard Branson-backed hyperloop startup that ran the first passenger hyperloop test in 2020 but pivoted away from passenger transport and laid off half its staff in 2022, effectively ending its original mission.
lesson: Novel transportation modes face such steep regulatory and infrastructure hurdles that even successful tech demos don't guarantee commercialization.

name: Arrivo
summary: Hyperloop-adjacent high-speed ground transportation startup founded by a former Hyperloop One executive that shut down in 2018 after failing to secure a route deal in Colorado.
lesson: Infrastructure-dependent transportation startups need a committed government partner before spending down capital on engineering.

name: Grow (Grin + Yellow)
summary: Latin American bike and scooter-sharing giant formed by the merger of Brazil's Yellow and Mexico's Grin that shut down operations across the region in 2020 amid the COVID-19 demand collapse and cash shortages.
lesson: Merging two cash-burning competitors doesn't fix the underlying unit economics, especially heading into a demand shock.

name: Bounce
summary: Indian dockless scooter-rental startup (formerly Metro Bikes) that shut down its core rental business in 2022 to pivot fully into electric-vehicle manufacturing.
lesson: Two-wheeler rental economics in price-sensitive markets pushed the company to abandon its original business entirely.

name: Vogo
summary: Bangalore-based dockless scooter rental startup backed by Ola that wound down operations in 2022 as parent Ola restructured its mobility investments.
lesson: A startup dependent on a single strategic investor is vulnerable to that investor's own portfolio triage decisions.

name: REEF Technology
summary: Parking-lot-turned-logistics-hub company (originally ParkJockey) that raised a huge SoftBank-led round at a $12.6B valuation but sold off major parking and delivery-kitchen units in 2023 amid deep losses.
lesson: Pivoting a parking-lot network into multiple unrelated logistics businesses simultaneously diluted focus and burned cash.

name: Coord
summary: Curb-space and parking-data startup spun out of Sidewalk Labs that wound down in 2022 after failing to build a large enough commercial base for city curb-management data.
lesson: City infrastructure data products can have long sales cycles that outlast a startup's runway.

name: Slync
summary: Freight and supply-chain orchestration software startup that shut down in 2023 after its founder and CEO were criminally charged with defrauding investors.
lesson: Founder fraud can vaporize a well-funded logistics-tech company overnight, regardless of the underlying product.

name: Scoot Networks
summary: San Francisco electric scooter and moped-sharing pioneer that was acquired by Bird in 2019, giving early investors a clean exit as the sector consolidated.
lesson: Being acquired at the right moment ahead of a market shakeout can be a better outcome than trying to outlast bigger rivals.

name: Motivate
summary: Operator of major US bike-share systems including Citi Bike, Divvy and Bay Wheels that was acquired by Lyft in 2018 for roughly $250M, becoming the backbone of Lyft's bike and scooter division.
lesson: Owning the operational contracts for city-run bike-share systems created a durable, less commoditized business than dockless free-floating fleets.

name: Zipcar
summary: Pioneer of hourly car-sharing that IPO'd in 2011 and was acquired by Avis Budget Group in 2013 for about $500M, remaining the most enduring car-sharing brand in the US.
lesson: Being the category-defining first mover in car-sharing let Zipcar reach a profitable acquisition exit years before ride-hailing disrupted the space.

name: Turo
summary: Peer-to-peer car-sharing marketplace (the 'Airbnb of cars') that has grown to millions of trips annually and filed confidentially for an IPO, outlasting rival Getaround's public-market collapse.
lesson: An asset-light marketplace model proved more durable in car-sharing than capital-intensive owned-fleet competitors.

name: HopSkipDrive
summary: Ride-hailing service specializing in safe transportation for children, focused on school districts and youth in foster care, that has scaled to serve hundreds of school districts nationwide.
lesson: Focusing on an underserved, trust-sensitive niche (kids' transport) with enterprise (school district) contracts created a defensible business ride-hailing giants ignored.

name: Zoox
summary: Purpose-built robotaxi startup with a bidirectional, steering-wheel-free vehicle design that was acquired by Amazon in 2020 for roughly $1.3B and continues developing driverless rides in Las Vegas and San Francisco.
lesson: A distinctive purpose-built vehicle design gave Zoox a defensible niche that made it attractive to a deep-pocketed acquirer before it ran out of cash.

name: Waymo
summary: Alphabet's self-driving car unit (spun out of the Google Self-Driving Car Project) now operates paid robotaxi service in multiple US cities including Phoenix, San Francisco, Los Angeles and Austin, the clear AV market leader.
lesson: Sustained, patient capital from a deep-pocketed parent allowed Waymo to out-endure nearly every AV competitor.

name: Nuro
summary: Autonomous delivery-vehicle startup founded by ex-Google engineers that has run driverless grocery and retail delivery pilots with Kroger, Domino's and Walmart, remaining one of the best-funded AV startups still operating.
lesson: Focusing on lower-stakes delivery autonomy rather than passenger robotaxis reduced regulatory and safety risk relative to rivals.

name: Pony.ai
summary: Chinese-American robotaxi startup operating driverless ride-hailing in Beijing, Guangzhou and other cities that completed a Nasdaq IPO in 2024.
lesson: Building deep government relationships in China gave Pony.ai a regulatory pathway that many Western AV rivals lacked.

name: Rivian
summary: Electric pickup truck and SUV maker backed by Amazon and Ford that IPO'd in 2021 in one of the largest US IPOs ever and continues shipping vehicles including Amazon's electric delivery vans.
lesson: Landing a large anchor customer (Amazon's delivery van order) gave Rivian a revenue backstop most EV startups never secured.

name: Lucid Motors
summary: Luxury EV maker that went public via SPAC in 2021 and continues producing the Lucid Air sedan and Gravity SUV, sustained by billions in backing from Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund.
lesson: A single deep-pocketed sovereign-wealth backer can carry an EV maker through years of losses that would sink less-capitalized rivals.

name: SpotHero
summary: Parking reservation marketplace connecting drivers with garages and lots in major US cities that has remained an independent, profitable-trending leader in parking tech.
lesson: An asset-light marketplace connecting existing parking supply with demand avoided the capital intensity that sank many mobility hardware startups.

name: ParkWhiz
summary: One of the earliest parking-reservation platforms, later merged into REEF Technology's mobility division, that continues to operate as a parking-booking marketplace serving major venues and airports.
lesson: A capital-light marketplace model let ParkWhiz survive even as its later corporate parent, REEF, struggled elsewhere.

name: ParkMobile
summary: Mobile parking-payment app used across hundreds of North American cities that was acquired by Sweden's EasyPark Group in 2021, forming one of the largest digital parking networks globally.
lesson: Deep integration with municipal parking authorities created switching costs that made ParkMobile a strategic acquisition target.

name: Grab
summary: Southeast Asian ride-hailing and super-app that acquired Uber's regional operations in 2018 and went public via the largest SPAC merger in history in 2021, dominating ride-hailing and delivery across the region.
lesson: Acquiring Uber's regional business in exchange for equity let Grab consolidate market leadership rather than fight a prolonged subsidy war.

name: Careem
summary: Dubai-based ride-hailing 'super app' that dominated the Middle East and was acquired by Uber in 2019 for $3.1 billion, one of the largest tech acquisitions in the region's history.
lesson: Deep localization for regional payment and cultural norms let Careem out-compete Uber locally before Uber simply bought it.

name: Bolt (Taxify)
summary: Estonian ride-hailing and micromobility company that expanded across Europe and Africa with a low-cost model, reaching a multi-billion-dollar valuation while remaining independent of Uber and Lyft.
lesson: Undercutting Uber on commission and pricing in secondary European and African markets Uber deprioritized created durable market share.

name: Didi Global
summary: China's dominant ride-hailing company that acquired Uber China in 2016 and became the world's largest ride-hailing platform by trips, despite a 2021 Chinese regulatory crackdown that forced its delisting from the NYSE.
lesson: Domestic market dominance can survive severe regulatory shocks, though it may force retreat from global capital markets.

name: Ola Cabs
summary: India's largest domestic ride-hailing company that outcompeted Uber for market share in its home country, though its UK, Australia and New Zealand international expansions were later scaled back or exited.
lesson: Winning decisively at home doesn't guarantee the same playbook transfers to international markets with different regulatory and competitive dynamics.

name: VeoRide
summary: US-based shared e-scooter and e-bike operator that grew steadily and profitably across mid-sized US cities and college campuses while many larger, flashier rivals like Bird and Tier collapsed.
lesson: Prioritizing profitability and steady mid-market city contracts over blitzscaling into every major metro helped Veo avoid its rivals' cash-burn traps.

name: Voi Technology
summary: Swedish e-scooter operator that has remained one of the few profitable-trending micromobility companies in Europe, focused on safety features and city-government partnerships.
lesson: Prioritizing city regulator relationships and safety compliance in Europe helped Voi outlast better-funded but less-compliant rivals.

name: Dott
summary: Amsterdam-based shared e-scooter and e-bike operator that expanded profitably across major European cities and acquired parts of failed rival Wind Mobility's operations.
lesson: Selective city entry and municipal-partnership discipline in Europe's tightly regulated scooter market proved more durable than blitzscaling.

name: Neuron Mobility
summary: Singapore-founded e-scooter operator focused on Australia, New Zealand, UK and Canada that has grown by prioritizing safety tech and close city-government partnerships in markets larger rivals ignored.
lesson: Focusing on English-speaking secondary markets overlooked by Bird and Lime let Neuron build durable regional leadership.

name: Cabify
summary: Spain-founded ride-hailing company focused on Spanish and Latin American markets that has remained independent and profitable-trending by avoiding a head-on subsidy war with Uber in its core Spanish-speaking markets.
lesson: Concentrating on Spanish-language markets with strong local regulatory relationships let Cabify avoid the capital-burning global expansion race.

name: Gojek
summary: Indonesian ride-hailing and super-app pioneer (motorbike taxis, delivery, payments) that merged with e-commerce giant Tokopedia in 2021 to form GoTo Group, one of Southeast Asia's largest tech companies.
lesson: Building a multi-service super-app around a core ride-hailing product created cross-selling network effects that a single-service rival couldn't match.

name: inDrive
summary: Kazakhstan-founded ride-hailing platform using a unique 'name your price' bidding model that expanded to over 700 cities across emerging markets in Latin America, Africa and Asia, becoming one of the most-downloaded ride apps globally.
lesson: A differentiated pricing model (rider-set fares) let inDrive win price-sensitive emerging markets that standard surge-pricing apps struggled to penetrate profitably.

name: BlaBlaCar
summary: French long-distance carpooling marketplace connecting drivers and passengers for intercity trips that has scaled profitably across Europe, Latin America and India without the capital intensity of owned-fleet models.
lesson: Matching existing car trips instead of operating a vehicle fleet gave BlaBlaCar structurally lower capital needs than ride-hailing rivals.

name: Via
summary: On-demand shared shuttle and transit-technology company that pivoted from a consumer ridesharing app into a B2G software provider powering microtransit for hundreds of transit agencies worldwide, ahead of a planned IPO.
lesson: Pivoting from consumer subsidy-driven rides to selling software to transit agencies created a far more durable, less cash-intensive business model.

name: Wunder Mobility
summary: German B2B software platform providing the technology backend for car-sharing, scooter and moped operators worldwide, letting it profit from micromobility's growth without owning risky vehicle fleets itself.
lesson: Selling picks-and-shovels software to fleet operators avoided the fleet-ownership risk that sank many of its own customers.

name: Envoy Technologies
summary: Electric car-sharing company focused on multifamily housing communities and universities that has grown steadily by embedding EV fleets directly into residential and campus properties rather than competing citywide.
lesson: Targeting a captive niche (residential communities) rather than open citywide competition avoided the fleet-scale capital battles that killed Car2Go and Maven.

name: Revel
summary: NYC-founded electric moped-sharing startup that pivoted its core business toward building fast-charging EV superhub stations and an EV rideshare fleet after moped operations proved difficult to scale profitably.
lesson: Recognizing that moped-sharing alone wasn't scalable and pivoting into EV charging infrastructure let Revel capture a growing adjacent market.

name: Moovit
summary: Israeli urban mobility and public-transit navigation app used by hundreds of millions of riders that was acquired by Intel in 2020 for roughly $900 million to support Intel's Mobileye autonomous driving unit.
lesson: Aggregating public transit data at massive scale made Moovit a strategic asset for autonomous-driving mapping, not just a consumer app.

name: Gogoro
summary: Taiwanese electric scooter and battery-swapping network operator that built a widely adopted swap-station infrastructure across Taiwan and expanded to other Asian markets, going public via SPAC in 2022.
lesson: Solving battery-swap infrastructure at national scale in a scooter-dense market (unlike Better Place's failed car-focused attempt) created a defensible network.

name: NIO
summary: Chinese premium EV maker known for its battery-swap stations and NIO Houses retail concept that has scaled to become one of China's leading EV brands and listed on the NYSE.
lesson: Combining a strong domestic government-backed capital base with a differentiated battery-swap service model let NIO survive China's brutal EV price war.

name: XPeng
summary: Chinese smart EV maker known for its in-house autonomous driving software that has become one of China's top EV exporters, listed on both the NYSE and Hong Kong exchange.
lesson: Investing heavily in proprietary software (autonomy, infotainment) differentiated XPeng from hardware-only EV competitors in a crowded Chinese market.

name: Free2Move
summary: Stellantis's multi-brand mobility platform offering car-sharing, subscriptions and rentals across Europe and North America that has continued operating and expanding even as rival automaker mobility units like Car2Go and Maven shut down.
lesson: Aggregating multiple mobility services (rather than operating one capital-intensive fleet model) gave Free2Move more flexibility to adjust to weak segments.

name: Aurora Innovation
summary: Autonomous trucking company founded by ex-Google, Tesla and Uber ATG leaders that acquired Uber's self-driving unit in 2020, went public via SPAC in 2021, and launched driverless commercial freight hauls in Texas in 2024 while rivals TuSimple and Embark collapsed.
lesson: Surviving the AV trucking shakeout required both deep technical credibility and enough capital discipline to outlast a wave of competitor bankruptcies.

name: Passport Inc.
summary: Parking, mobility and curbside-management software provider serving hundreds of North American cities and universities that has scaled as a B2G SaaS platform without owning vehicles or physical infrastructure.
lesson: Selling government-facing SaaS for curb and parking management sidestepped the capital intensity that sank hardware-and-fleet mobility startups.
